Goals for this Presentation


Understand at a basic level what AI is
Understand that there are different kinds of AI models
Understand different AI models are appropriate for different tasks
Be able to apply this information to using AI or writing an AI policy
Understand how ETDD’s policy addresses potential issues and be able to adapt for your organization
Have a basis for further learning

title: AI Policy
text:
What Is AI?



How would you define AI?




“AI is whatever hasn’t been done yet.” — Tesler’s Theorem1




Larry Tesler was a computer scientist who worked in the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (and later pioneered copy and paste as we know them today)

title: AI Policy
text:
Defining AI

How do you define “Intelligence”?
Academic definitions differ and are not always helpful for practical use



AI Definition Overview2








Human-based
Ideal Rationality




Reasoning-based
Systems that think like humans
Systems that think rationally


Behavior-based
Systems that act like humans
Systems that act rationally





In general, it’s somewhat common to not have clear definitions in academic fields because it’s difficult to create a definition that covers everything it should while excluding everything else. As an exercise after this presentation, try to define a ‘chair’ in a way that would include e.g. beanbag chairs, but not horses. AI does have a harder time with the definition of the field in part because as a field it interacts with computer science, statistics, philosophy, neurology, neurocomputation, and other fields. Depending on one’s focus, AI can be interpreted in wildly different ways, and that’s without accounting for academic vs common-use definitions.

title: AI Policy
text:
Defining AI
AI is “an engineered or machine-based system that can, for a given set of objectives, generate outputs such as predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. AI systems are designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy”3

This is the definition we’re using at ETDD

title: AI Policy
text:
Defining AI
Questions to help determine if it’s AI:

Does the technology use data to provide predictions, recommendations, insights, or decisions?
Does the technology augment or replace human decision-making?
Does the company use words such as “personalized”, “tailored”, and “adaptive” in its marketing?
Would it be difficult or impossible to write out how a program does what it does, either because the process is opaque or because it is mathematically complex? 4


These are in ETDD’s AI policy. If the answer is yes to any of them, you should treat it as AI until told otherwise.

When thinking of the different kinds of AI, it might help to think of them like Excel vs Word. You probably don’t know much about how either works at a low level (i.e. what language they’re coded in, how the data is represented), but you know you probably shouldn’t use Word to create tables with complex calculations or Excel to write an essay.

title: AI Policy
section: Real Life Examples of Each Kind of AI
text:
Real Life Examples of Each Kind of AI




Logicist


Probabilistic


Neurocomputational






 Optical Character Recognition




 Video Games7



 Spam Filters8




Lots of video games use AI to control NPCs (non-playable characters). For example, in Skyrim NPCs have to know where to walk without someone controlling them, how do they do that? How do NPCs know how much they should charge for a book, or spend on a sword you want to sell them? Newer video games often use a combination of several kinds of AI, so perhaps in Skyrim there would be a logicist AI to determine where NPCs can walk, and then a neurocomputational AI to determine where they actually walk.
Unfortunately, spam emails don’t usually tell you they’re spam, so how can spam filters work? It would take hours and hours of manual work to create rules that would work for the spam I get on my work email and my personal email, much less that would be mostly accurate for most people. This is somewhere ML excels- select an appropriate model, give it labelled data, and it can create rules to catch most spam.
Optical Character Recognition is used to e.g. add words to scanned PDFs. It’s usually based on Neural nets, which are especially good at computer vision

title: AI Policy
text:
AI Model Type Overview









Logicist
Probabilistic
Neurocomputational




good for:
clearly defined logic
clear factors
No clear process


bad at:
handling uncertainty
operating with limited /bad data or assumptions
Truth; Explainability


example:
email inbox rules
(some) population forecasting
chatGPT




To give another example of each: In my personal life, I’ve signed up for a few store rewards cards. I like looking at the flyers before I go, but I don’t want them cluttering up my inbox, so I set inbox rules to move those to a folder. Some of the companies use different emails, so I can’t create rules that always filter correctly because there is some uncertainty about what emails should / should not be moved.
Car retailers often try to predict how many cars they will sell of certain types so they can buy appropriate stock. There aren’t clear, human definable rules for predicting that, but they have previous sales data and know they can looks at things like SUV vs truck and Honda vs Ford. The quality of data sets an upper limit on how good the forecast can be. Some of them artificially inflate demand in December by running sales, so they have to account for that outside of the Machine Learning model because the data is not accurate for December.
Computer vision uses neural nets to do things like determine if a picture is of a cat or a dog. A more practical example is to sorting old, hand-drawn paperwork. There are typically no clearly defined features to use, and no clear rules about which is which you could communicate to a computer. You can cross-train a computer vision neural network to sort the documents into different folders, but it can’t tell you e.g. why it sorted them a certain way.

title: AI Policy
text:
Quick Clarifications

AI is not just Large Language Models9
AI is not new10
It is still valuable to learn new skills


AI includes more than just Large Language Models like CoPilot, it also includes things like Spell Check or the algorithms Walmart uses to know how many oranges they should order. When talking about AI (especially in the context of writing a policy), it is important to keep this in mind.
AI is not new, but it might feel that way because the history of it is full of booms and busts. We figure out something super cool, and then we over promise and underdeliver. When we haven’t solved all of the world’s problems in five years people lose interest in AI and decide all of it is useless. Then things that objectively are AI stop being called AI because otherwise they won’t get funding/interest/etc.
With the rise of new AI tools, it is still valuable to learn new skills. If you need to use complicated formulas in a spreadsheet you might be able to get CoPilot to do it for you, but if you take the time to understand the basics of what is going on you might find a better way to do it, and it’ll be easier if you have to do it again/fix it if it breaks. On the other hand, if you’re already an Excel wizard, the skill that you might find helpful is learning to get CoPilot to fix formulas when they break.

title: AI Policy
text:
Quick Advice for Using AI

Don’t use AI search results without verifying them
If you use chat bots:

Give them context in your prompt
Try different prompting strategies (e.g. say “you are an X”, be nice)

Don’t forget the sunk cost fallacy11
Sometimes older methods are better


AI is not a replacement for verified sources- the little robots know what a fact looks like, not what is true or not. If you e.g. look up statistics with ChatGPT, the robots will give you something that looks accurate, but you will have to double check with a reputable source (probably data.census.gov). If someone builds an AI tool that will pull census data, the source is not the tool the source will be wherever the tool gets the data from (likely data.census.gov) but you should still double check because mistakes can happen when coding things like that.
Currently, there is not clear evidence that certain strategies are universally helpful with all tasks for any given LLM model, and the models are changing at a fast enough pace that a strategy that works now might not a month from now. Instead, try different strategies to see which seems to get you what you want. Some strategies include being polite in how you word the prompt; including in your prompt something like “You are a planner,”; and prompting it to “think step by step”. This last one will use more resources, and is not always effective, so use it sparingly.
It can be easy to spend more time trying to do something an ‘easy’ way than you would doing it the ‘hard’ way. I have a tendency to do this, and what I’ve found helpful is setting a timer for a quarter or half as long as I think it’d take to do the hard way and switching to the hard way if I’m not done in that time.
AI is the new cool shiny toy that’s suddenly everywhere, and it can be exciting to use new tech (or old tech with a new coat of paint). It’s not always the most effective or easiest way to do things even if it feels like it is. There are times where AI is not the best solution. If you want to check the accessibility of a website, there is a lot you can do with automation (e.g. color contrast), but some of it is easier to just check than try to create AI for (full screen-reader compatibility).

title: AI Policy
section: Why an AI policy is imporant
text:
Why an AI policy is imporant

More people want to use AI
Many people don’t know how to responsibly use AI
Some AI is cutting edge technology treated as traditional software
Rapid changes in legal and technical realms
A well-written policy can save you time and headaches later


You don’t need a policy for something no one wants to use, but increasingly people want to use AI but don’t necessarily know how to properly use AI. There is a lot of conflicting and misinformation about what AI is and how to use it. Part of the issue is that AI describes a wide range of things, including some cutting-edge technology that’s being treated as if it is traditional, well-established software. One area where you see some of the potential issues with it is that the legal situation and technical capabilities are rapidly changing. A well-written policy now might save you time and issues later, whether it’s not needing to rewrite your policy a few months from now due to something like a new law or potentially saving you from legal issues due to someone unintentionally using AI in a way that is unethical or illegal.

title: AI Policy
text:
Rapid Changes






Policy Considerations



Legal12
Technical

Unique hacking vectors13
Coding14

Consumer-Facing





The legal realm around AI is currently rapidly changing and uncertain. A lot of where we’re currently seeing these changes is with figuring out how current laws apply to new(er) AI tools. For example, multiple AI Notetakers are in court trying to determine things like if collecting biometric information without proper consent is breaking California privacy law, or if sending an AI notetaker to a meeting you don’t attend might be breaking federal wire tap law. In the technical realm generative AI is subject to unique ways of hacking traditional software is not, and cybersecurity experts are just starting to get a sense of the scope of the problem. One area of particular interest is with coding- if you use generative AI to code there are several ways it can pose cybersecurity risks that are elevated compared to coding by hand. Even if you don’t open CoPilot and ask it to code for you, if you use low or no code tools, most of those use generative AI. Coding is also a great example of how rapidly things change- since ETDD’s policy was written, the primary way of using generative AI for coding has completely changed. Even when what is legal to do or technically possible to do doesn’t change, what is available to you is probably changing. A lot of the tools you use (e.g. Microsoft Word) are probably adding in AI abilities that were not available until recently.

title: AI Policy
text:
Rapid Changes







ETDD’s Policy



Guiding Principles
Focus on flexible processes and risk assessments
Specific as needed (e.g. AI Note takers, generating code)
Separate Generative AI Policy
Updating regularly






We include our Guiding Principles so when something changes (e.g. a new way of using AI is available, we lose access to an existing tool), we aren’t starting from square one. We already know what we care about and just have to determine how the change fits in to our existing framework. We don’t include a list of approved AI tools or use cases because it is likely things would change and then we’d have to rewrite the policy. Instead, we focus on flexible processes and risk assessments that can adapt as things change. We keep most of the policy general (e.g. all staff must be trained before using AI), but in some cases we have additional rules (e.g. AI Notetakers require consent before using, we don’t allow AI generated code) where they are needed. This lack of specificity when it isn’t needed allows us to not have to rewrite the policy as often. Generative AI is where we’re seeing a lot of these changes, so it is a seperate policy. Right now we review the AI Policy at least once per year, but can update it sooner if needed.

title: AI Policy
text:
Lack of Accessible Information






Policy Considerations



Most information is very polarized
Most tools don’t tell you how they work15
Difficult to know if something will do what it says
Difficult to know if something has unstated risks
Lots of start ups





Most of the information out there is either very pro-AI or anti-AI, but you need a nuanced understanding of AI to write an effective policy. Most of the AI tools available don’t tell you how they work, so you don’t have a way to know if it will actually do what is says or if it has unstated risks (e.g. does that “AI coding” tool use templates or an LLM? Do you need to worry about potential malware with it?). These issues are heighted by the proliferation of start ups in the space. There are start ups run by competent, passionate people who are honest about what their product is and what it can do, but there are also start ups run by people who just care about the money who are incentivized to tell you whatever it takes for you to buy their product. Even if you find a great start up, there’s no guarantee that it’ll be here and you’ll be able to use it a year from now, and if you don’t know how it works it’ll be difficult to find a suitable alternative.

title: AI Policy
text:
Lack of Accessible Information







ETDD’s Policy



Guiding Principles help indicate how and why
AI Training is required
AI Request process
Risk Assessments
Transparency






Guiding Principles help staff understand how they are meant to interact with AI and why. All staff must complete AI Training to ensure they have the knowledge required to properly follow the AI Policy. Staff must submit AI Requests for the AI and Technology Manager to assess- this helps make sure they are using AI in a way that aligns with our policy. Risk Assessments help ensure the potential risks are known and accounted for, and might include asking providers for additional information about models if required. These assessments can help staff understand any restrictions on their use and why. This also moves the burden of understanding AI tools and reading privacy policies etc from staff who might not understand the underlying systems or what to be concerned about to the AI and Technology Manager who can more accurately assess these tools. ETDD prioritizes AI tools that are transparent about how they work, we are transparent about how we use AI, and if we develop any AI tools we will be transparent about how they work.

title: AI Policy
text:
Public Opinion






Policy Considerations



Public opinion divided16
Difficult to know potential consequences on relationships
Plagiarism17





Right now public opinion is very divided- people tend to be either very pro or anti AI, and so it’s difficult to know how using AI might affect your relationships with your agencies, your communities, and anyone else you work with. One area of particular interest is with plagiarism- as of right now there’s no consensus on if using generative AI constitutes plagiarism, and if so at what point.

title: AI Policy
text:
Public Opinion







ETDD’s Policy



Proactively disclose our use of generative AI
Cite usage as appropriate
Avoid certain high-risk use cases
Most Risk Assessments consider this






To try to navigate this, at ETDD we proactively disclose our use of generative AI in a way that is appropriate to the use-case. For example, if you edit an email for succinctness you’d say “this email was edited with CoPilot for succinctness”. Or if you generated a transcript and then edited it you’d say “these meeting notes were generated by MS Transcribe, and fact-checked and edited by ETDD staff.” ETDD avoids certain high-risk use cases like using generative AI in hiring decisions which could cause a scandal or potentially legal issues depending on how badly things go. While it’s not in the policy, most risk assessments consider public opinion.

title: AI Policy
text:
Privacy and Data Concerns






Policy Considerations



AI Note Takers18
Data leaks
Sensitive Information leaks19
FOIA





Thinking about interacting with the public, privacy rights are a potentially major issue. Several AI Note Takers are currently in court trying to determine if they’ve violated privacy laws, and even if they haven’t a lot of people don’t want their biometric information collected without warning. Even when you’re not dealing with privacy violations, you have to worry about data leaks. Let’s say you’re in a meeting, and you’ve got a problem with one of the mayors holding up a project. And you’re honest about the problem because you need to figure out what to do. Someone in that meeting is using an AI Notetaker, and some time later that AI Notetaker has a data leak. The transcript from that meeting is now publicly available, and you could face consequences ranging from the loss of trust of your communities to potential legal consequences. And a lot of people put information into generative AI that they shouldn’t- people have put their user names and passwords, their social security numbers, all sorts of things that are now public due to data leaks. And even if you are careful about what you put in, if you give these tools access to your files, your emails, etc. it could leak anything contained in those. As a development district, anything you put into generative AI might be subject to a Freedom of Information Act Request- if this is relevant to you go ask a lawyer.

title: AI Policy
text:
Privacy and Data Concerns







ETDD’s Policy



Don’t put information not ready for public in generative AI
Always ask for consent before using an AI Note Taker






To avoid having to ask a lawyer (and avoid the risk of data leaks), we don’t put any information that is not ready for the public into generative AI. Also, we always ask for consent before adding an AI Note Taker to a meeting, or recording anything with the intent to put it through generative AI after the fact. It is rude to record people without consent, and while Tennessee is a one-party consent state, we work with people in other states where it might be illegal to record them without consent.

title: AI Policy
section: Lack of Resources Tailored for Development Districts
text:
Lack of Resources Tailored for Development Districts






Policy Considerations



Difficult to find relevant trainings
Difficult to find resources on AI policies

Most is aimed at people who are developing AI

Time sink





Because resources for development districts don’t already exist, you will have to create ones from scratch. However you decide to write and implement an AI Policy, keep in mind it will take time. For example, when I recieve an AI request I have to spend time to figure out how someone is planning on using AI, why they’re using it, if the tool they are requesting is appropriate both for what they want and for us to use, then if it’s not I have to provide an alternative. If you want to provide blanket tools and uses you might spend time approving tools people don’t want or later having to figure out who is using it and how to handle it if there is an issue with the tool. At ETDD we approve things at the level of per person per tool per Use Case, in part to avoid wasting time researching tools for functions that haven’t been requested and to make sure people know what the policy is.

title: AI Policy
text:
Putting Guiding Principles into Policy






Policy Considerations



Translating Guiding Principles into policy is hard





Even once you decide what you care about with AI, it can be difficult to figure out e.g. what using AI effectively should mean as a policy.

title: AI Policy
text:
Putting Guiding Principles into Policy







ETDD’s Policy


Example using Effectiveness:

AI is a tool not the tool

No data analysis with generative AI

Automation Bias20
Resource Use21






To be effective with AI, it is important to use the correct tool. This might sometimes mean using older AI tools, and often it will mean not using AI. One area in particular where some people want to use AI and shouldn’t is when it comes to using generative AI for anything factual. In ETDD’s policy we specifically state that we will not use generative AI for any form of data analysis (the tiny little robots know what a fact looks like, not what a fact is) because if you get the correct answer it will be because you got lucky.
When using AI, you have to make sure you are still outputting something that is up to the quality standards. We tend to expect computers to be smarter/more competent than we are- I think most of us have typed something in Word and gotten a spell check error, and even if you were pretty confident you spelled it correctly you probably got out your dictionary to check. It is easy to think what the computer puts out is always better than what you would create; at an extreme this can include even things like trusting information you get from generative AI more than an expert, but it can also include not being as thorough when you fact check things you get from AI because it feels more trustworthy. This is part of why you have to check any information with verified sources (also because the little robots don’t understand what a ‘fact’ is). If a model produces a bad result (e.g. bad image, inaccurate transcript, inaccurate information), you are responsible for anything that happens if you use it. AI is a tool and you are using it, it is not autonomous (yet)
While it’s less obvious when it’s on the cloud (on someone else’s computer), some AI models (generative AI in particular) use more resources than other options, and this sometimes translates to a higher cost to you. To be effective, you should balance your benefit with the increased resource use and try to find ways to reduce the increased resource use where possible.

title: AI Policy
text:
Practical Advice for Policies

Be prepared for technical issues
Adapt for your organization
Consider how this might fit with other policies
AI is not always traditional software
Try to provide alternatives


If you have an AI incident, there is a chance it will be because someone accidentally signed up for an AI tool, or tried to disconnect it from their calendar but had a technical issue. You might be thinking that the way ETDD does things sounds like the best method, but if it involves any paperwork it will absolutely not happen at your district. If so, figure out how to adapt for your organization. You probably already have a tech policy, but your tech policy almost certainly does not cover everything you need. AI is often not just on your computer, most of the user-facing options are also online, so a policy about downloading things will have low relevance If you have to say no to something, try to understand the need to find a suitable alternative. That’ll help address genuine needs and help improve buy-in to the policy.

title: AI Policy
text:
What we’ll cover:

What AI is
Examples of AI in your life
How AI works (conceptually)
ETDD’s Policy (what and why)
Examples of how to put this information into action

title: AI Policy
text:
Goals for this Presentation


Understand at a basic level what AI is
Understand that there are different kinds of AI models
Understand different AI models are appropriate for different tasks
Be able to apply this information to using AI or writing an AI policy
Understand how ETDD’s policy addresses potential issues and be able to adapt for your organization
Have a basis for further learning

title: AI Policy
text:
What Is AI?



How would you define AI?




“AI is whatever hasn’t been done yet.” — Tesler’s Theorem1




Larry Tesler was a computer scientist who worked in the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (and later pioneered copy and paste as we know them today)

title: AI Policy
text:
Defining AI

How do you define “Intelligence”?
Academic definitions differ and are not always helpful for practical use



AI Definition Overview2








Human-based
Ideal Rationality




Reasoning-based
Systems that think like humans
Systems that think rationally


Behavior-based
Systems that act like humans
Systems that act rationally





In general, it’s somewhat common to not have clear definitions in academic fields because it’s difficult to create a definition that covers everything it should while excluding everything else. As an exercise after this presentation, try to define a ‘chair’ in a way that would include e.g. beanbag chairs, but not horses. AI does have a harder time with the definition of the field in part because as a field it interacts with computer science, statistics, philosophy, neurology, neurocomputation, and other fields. Depending on one’s focus, AI can be interpreted in wildly different ways, and that’s without accounting for academic vs common-use definitions.

title: AI Policy
text:
Defining AI
AI is “an engineered or machine-based system that can, for a given set of objectives, generate outputs such as predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. AI systems are designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy”3

This is the definition we’re using at ETDD

title: AI Policy
text:
Defining AI
Questions to help determine if it’s AI:

Does the technology use data to provide predictions, recommendations, insights, or decisions?
Does the technology augment or replace human decision-making?
Does the company use words such as “personalized”, “tailored”, and “adaptive” in its marketing?
Would it be difficult or impossible to write out how a program does what it does, either because the process is opaque or because it is mathematically complex? 4


These are in ETDD’s AI policy. If the answer is yes to any of them, you should treat it as AI until told otherwise.

title: AI Policy
text:
Examples of AI



What are some examples of AI you interact with on a regular basis?




 Optical Character Recognition


 Video Games5


 Spam Filters6

title: AI Policy
text:
How AI Works Conceptually


Understanding the different kinds can help you know when and how to use AI










G


AI

AI



Logistic

Logistic



AI->Logistic





NonLogistic

NonLogistic



AI->NonLogistic





Probabilistic

Probabilistic



NonLogistic->Probabilistic





Neurocomputational

Neurocomputational

When thinking of the different kinds of AI, it might help to think of them like Excel vs Word. You probably don’t know much about how either works at a low level (i.e. what language they’re coded in, how the data is represented), but you know you probably shouldn’t use Word to create tables with complex calculations or Excel to write an essay.

title: AI Policy
section: Real Life Examples of Each Kind of AI
text:
Real Life Examples of Each Kind of AI




Logicist


Probabilistic


Neurocomputational






 Optical Character Recognition




 Video Games7



 Spam Filters8




Lots of video games use AI to control NPCs (non-playable characters). For example, in Skyrim NPCs have to know where to walk without someone controlling them, how do they do that? How do NPCs know how much they should charge for a book, or spend on a sword you want to sell them? Newer video games often use a combination of several kinds of AI, so perhaps in Skyrim there would be a logicist AI to determine where NPCs can walk, and then a neurocomputational AI to determine where they actually walk.
Unfortunately, spam emails don’t usually tell you they’re spam, so how can spam filters work? It would take hours and hours of manual work to create rules that would work for the spam I get on my work email and my personal email, much less that would be mostly accurate for most people. This is somewhere ML excels- select an appropriate model, give it labelled data, and it can create rules to catch most spam.
Optical Character Recognition is used to e.g. add words to scanned PDFs. It’s usually based on Neural nets, which are especially good at computer vision

title: AI Policy
text:
AI Model Type Overview









Logicist
Probabilistic
Neurocomputational




good for:
clearly defined logic
clear factors
No clear process


bad at:
handling uncertainty
operating with limited /bad data or assumptions
Truth; Explainability


example:
email inbox rules
(some) population forecasting
chatGPT




To give another example of each: In my personal life, I’ve signed up for a few store rewards cards. I like looking at the flyers before I go, but I don’t want them cluttering up my inbox, so I set inbox rules to move those to a folder. Some of the companies use different emails, so I can’t create rules that always filter correctly because there is some uncertainty about what emails should / should not be moved.
Car retailers often try to predict how many cars they will sell of certain types so they can buy appropriate stock. There aren’t clear, human definable rules for predicting that, but they have previous sales data and know they can looks at things like SUV vs truck and Honda vs Ford. The quality of data sets an upper limit on how good the forecast can be. Some of them artificially inflate demand in December by running sales, so they have to account for that outside of the Machine Learning model because the data is not accurate for December.
Computer vision uses neural nets to do things like determine if a picture is of a cat or a dog. A more practical example is to sorting old, hand-drawn paperwork. There are typically no clearly defined features to use, and no clear rules about which is which you could communicate to a computer. You can cross-train a computer vision neural network to sort the documents into different folders, but it can’t tell you e.g. why it sorted them a certain way.

title: AI Policy
text:
Quick Clarifications

AI is not just Large Language Models9
AI is not new10
It is still valuable to learn new skills


AI includes more than just Large Language Models like CoPilot, it also includes things like Spell Check or the algorithms Walmart uses to know how many oranges they should order. When talking about AI (especially in the context of writing a policy), it is important to keep this in mind.
AI is not new, but it might feel that way because the history of it is full of booms and busts. We figure out something super cool, and then we over promise and underdeliver. When we haven’t solved all of the world’s problems in five years people lose interest in AI and decide all of it is useless. Then things that objectively are AI stop being called AI because otherwise they won’t get funding/interest/etc.
With the rise of new AI tools, it is still valuable to learn new skills. If you need to use complicated formulas in a spreadsheet you might be able to get CoPilot to do it for you, but if you take the time to understand the basics of what is going on you might find a better way to do it, and it’ll be easier if you have to do it again/fix it if it breaks. On the other hand, if you’re already an Excel wizard, the skill that you might find helpful is learning to get CoPilot to fix formulas when they break.

title: AI Policy
text:
Quick Advice for Using AI

Don’t use AI search results without verifying them
If you use chat bots:

Give them context in your prompt
Try different prompting strategies (e.g. say “you are an X”, be nice)

Don’t forget the sunk cost fallacy11
Sometimes older methods are better


AI is not a replacement for verified sources- the little robots know what a fact looks like, not what is true or not. If you e.g. look up statistics with ChatGPT, the robots will give you something that looks accurate, but you will have to double check with a reputable source (probably data.census.gov). If someone builds an AI tool that will pull census data, the source is not the tool the source will be wherever the tool gets the data from (likely data.census.gov) but you should still double check because mistakes can happen when coding things like that.
Currently, there is not clear evidence that certain strategies are universally helpful with all tasks for any given LLM model, and the models are changing at a fast enough pace that a strategy that works now might not a month from now. Instead, try different strategies to see which seems to get you what you want. Some strategies include being polite in how you word the prompt; including in your prompt something like “You are a planner,”; and prompting it to “think step by step”. This last one will use more resources, and is not always effective, so use it sparingly.
It can be easy to spend more time trying to do something an ‘easy’ way than you would doing it the ‘hard’ way. I have a tendency to do this, and what I’ve found helpful is setting a timer for a quarter or half as long as I think it’d take to do the hard way and switching to the hard way if I’m not done in that time.
AI is the new cool shiny toy that’s suddenly everywhere, and it can be exciting to use new tech (or old tech with a new coat of paint). It’s not always the most effective or easiest way to do things even if it feels like it is. There are times where AI is not the best solution. If you want to check the accessibility of a website, there is a lot you can do with automation (e.g. color contrast), but some of it is easier to just check than try to create AI for (full screen-reader compatibility).

title: AI Policy
text:
Think about any potential questions:




Any questions you have about AI
Why an AI Policy is important
Anything specific you’re not sure how to handle

title: AI Policy
section: Why an AI policy is imporant
text:
Why an AI policy is imporant

More people want to use AI
Many people don’t know how to responsibly use AI
Some AI is cutting edge technology treated as traditional software
Rapid changes in legal and technical realms
A well-written policy can save you time and headaches later


You don’t need a policy for something no one wants to use, but increasingly people want to use AI but don’t necessarily know how to properly use AI. There is a lot of conflicting and misinformation about what AI is and how to use it. Part of the issue is that AI describes a wide range of things, including some cutting-edge technology that’s being treated as if it is traditional, well-established software. One area where you see some of the potential issues with it is that the legal situation and technical capabilities are rapidly changing. A well-written policy now might save you time and issues later, whether it’s not needing to rewrite your policy a few months from now due to something like a new law or potentially saving you from legal issues due to someone unintentionally using AI in a way that is unethical or illegal.

title: AI Policy
text:
ETDD’s Policy Outline


Introduction
Guiding Principles
Roles And Responsibilities
Definitions
General Information (and special notes)
Processes to review AI requests, sunset tools
Enforcement



A copy of ETDD’s policy is at the end of this presentation

title: AI Policy
text:
Rapid Changes






Policy Considerations



Legal12
Technical

Unique hacking vectors13
Coding14

Consumer-Facing





The legal realm around AI is currently rapidly changing and uncertain. A lot of where we’re currently seeing these changes is with figuring out how current laws apply to new(er) AI tools. For example, multiple AI Notetakers are in court trying to determine things like if collecting biometric information without proper consent is breaking California privacy law, or if sending an AI notetaker to a meeting you don’t attend might be breaking federal wire tap law. In the technical realm generative AI is subject to unique ways of hacking traditional software is not, and cybersecurity experts are just starting to get a sense of the scope of the problem. One area of particular interest is with coding- if you use generative AI to code there are several ways it can pose cybersecurity risks that are elevated compared to coding by hand. Even if you don’t open CoPilot and ask it to code for you, if you use low or no code tools, most of those use generative AI. Coding is also a great example of how rapidly things change- since ETDD’s policy was written, the primary way of using generative AI for coding has completely changed. Even when what is legal to do or technically possible to do doesn’t change, what is available to you is probably changing. A lot of the tools you use (e.g. Microsoft Word) are probably adding in AI abilities that were not available until recently.

title: AI Policy
text:
Rapid Changes







ETDD’s Policy



Guiding Principles
Focus on flexible processes and risk assessments
Specific as needed (e.g. AI Note takers, generating code)
Separate Generative AI Policy
Updating regularly






We include our Guiding Principles so when something changes (e.g. a new way of using AI is available, we lose access to an existing tool), we aren’t starting from square one. We already know what we care about and just have to determine how the change fits in to our existing framework. We don’t include a list of approved AI tools or use cases because it is likely things would change and then we’d have to rewrite the policy. Instead, we focus on flexible processes and risk assessments that can adapt as things change. We keep most of the policy general (e.g. all staff must be trained before using AI), but in some cases we have additional rules (e.g. AI Notetakers require consent before using, we don’t allow AI generated code) where they are needed. This lack of specificity when it isn’t needed allows us to not have to rewrite the policy as often. Generative AI is where we’re seeing a lot of these changes, so it is a seperate policy. Right now we review the AI Policy at least once per year, but can update it sooner if needed.

title: AI Policy
text:
Lack of Accessible Information






Policy Considerations



Most information is very polarized
Most tools don’t tell you how they work15
Difficult to know if something will do what it says
Difficult to know if something has unstated risks
Lots of start ups





Most of the information out there is either very pro-AI or anti-AI, but you need a nuanced understanding of AI to write an effective policy. Most of the AI tools available don’t tell you how they work, so you don’t have a way to know if it will actually do what is says or if it has unstated risks (e.g. does that “AI coding” tool use templates or an LLM? Do you need to worry about potential malware with it?). These issues are heighted by the proliferation of start ups in the space. There are start ups run by competent, passionate people who are honest about what their product is and what it can do, but there are also start ups run by people who just care about the money who are incentivized to tell you whatever it takes for you to buy their product. Even if you find a great start up, there’s no guarantee that it’ll be here and you’ll be able to use it a year from now, and if you don’t know how it works it’ll be difficult to find a suitable alternative.

title: AI Policy
text:
Lack of Accessible Information







ETDD’s Policy



Guiding Principles help indicate how and why
AI Training is required
AI Request process
Risk Assessments
Transparency






Guiding Principles help staff understand how they are meant to interact with AI and why. All staff must complete AI Training to ensure they have the knowledge required to properly follow the AI Policy. Staff must submit AI Requests for the AI and Technology Manager to assess- this helps make sure they are using AI in a way that aligns with our policy. Risk Assessments help ensure the potential risks are known and accounted for, and might include asking providers for additional information about models if required. These assessments can help staff understand any restrictions on their use and why. This also moves the burden of understanding AI tools and reading privacy policies etc from staff who might not understand the underlying systems or what to be concerned about to the AI and Technology Manager who can more accurately assess these tools. ETDD prioritizes AI tools that are transparent about how they work, we are transparent about how we use AI, and if we develop any AI tools we will be transparent about how they work.

title: AI Policy
text:
Public Opinion






Policy Considerations



Public opinion divided16
Difficult to know potential consequences on relationships
Plagiarism17





Right now public opinion is very divided- people tend to be either very pro or anti AI, and so it’s difficult to know how using AI might affect your relationships with your agencies, your communities, and anyone else you work with. One area of particular interest is with plagiarism- as of right now there’s no consensus on if using generative AI constitutes plagiarism, and if so at what point.

title: AI Policy
text:
Public Opinion







ETDD’s Policy



Proactively disclose our use of generative AI
Cite usage as appropriate
Avoid certain high-risk use cases
Most Risk Assessments consider this






To try to navigate this, at ETDD we proactively disclose our use of generative AI in a way that is appropriate to the use-case. For example, if you edit an email for succinctness you’d say “this email was edited with CoPilot for succinctness”. Or if you generated a transcript and then edited it you’d say “these meeting notes were generated by MS Transcribe, and fact-checked and edited by ETDD staff.” ETDD avoids certain high-risk use cases like using generative AI in hiring decisions which could cause a scandal or potentially legal issues depending on how badly things go. While it’s not in the policy, most risk assessments consider public opinion.

title: AI Policy
text:
Privacy and Data Concerns






Policy Considerations



AI Note Takers18
Data leaks
Sensitive Information leaks19
FOIA





Thinking about interacting with the public, privacy rights are a potentially major issue. Several AI Note Takers are currently in court trying to determine if they’ve violated privacy laws, and even if they haven’t a lot of people don’t want their biometric information collected without warning. Even when you’re not dealing with privacy violations, you have to worry about data leaks. Let’s say you’re in a meeting, and you’ve got a problem with one of the mayors holding up a project. And you’re honest about the problem because you need to figure out what to do. Someone in that meeting is using an AI Notetaker, and some time later that AI Notetaker has a data leak. The transcript from that meeting is now publicly available, and you could face consequences ranging from the loss of trust of your communities to potential legal consequences. And a lot of people put information into generative AI that they shouldn’t- people have put their user names and passwords, their social security numbers, all sorts of things that are now public due to data leaks. And even if you are careful about what you put in, if you give these tools access to your files, your emails, etc. it could leak anything contained in those. As a development district, anything you put into generative AI might be subject to a Freedom of Information Act Request- if this is relevant to you go ask a lawyer.

title: AI Policy
text:
Privacy and Data Concerns







ETDD’s Policy



Don’t put information not ready for public in generative AI
Always ask for consent before using an AI Note Taker






To avoid having to ask a lawyer (and avoid the risk of data leaks), we don’t put any information that is not ready for the public into generative AI. Also, we always ask for consent before adding an AI Note Taker to a meeting, or recording anything with the intent to put it through generative AI after the fact. It is rude to record people without consent, and while Tennessee is a one-party consent state, we work with people in other states where it might be illegal to record them without consent.

title: AI Policy
section: Lack of Resources Tailored for Development Districts
text:
Lack of Resources Tailored for Development Districts






Policy Considerations



Difficult to find relevant trainings
Difficult to find resources on AI policies

Most is aimed at people who are developing AI

Time sink





Because resources for development districts don’t already exist, you will have to create ones from scratch. However you decide to write and implement an AI Policy, keep in mind it will take time. For example, when I recieve an AI request I have to spend time to figure out how someone is planning on using AI, why they’re using it, if the tool they are requesting is appropriate both for what they want and for us to use, then if it’s not I have to provide an alternative. If you want to provide blanket tools and uses you might spend time approving tools people don’t want or later having to figure out who is using it and how to handle it if there is an issue with the tool. At ETDD we approve things at the level of per person per tool per Use Case, in part to avoid wasting time researching tools for functions that haven’t been requested and to make sure people know what the policy is.

title: AI Policy
text:
Putting Guiding Principles into Policy






Policy Considerations



Translating Guiding Principles into policy is hard





Even once you decide what you care about with AI, it can be difficult to figure out e.g. what using AI effectively should mean as a policy.

title: AI Policy
text:
Putting Guiding Principles into Policy







ETDD’s Policy


Example using Effectiveness:

AI is a tool not the tool

No data analysis with generative AI

Automation Bias20
Resource Use21






To be effective with AI, it is important to use the correct tool. This might sometimes mean using older AI tools, and often it will mean not using AI. One area in particular where some people want to use AI and shouldn’t is when it comes to using generative AI for anything factual. In ETDD’s policy we specifically state that we will not use generative AI for any form of data analysis (the tiny little robots know what a fact looks like, not what a fact is) because if you get the correct answer it will be because you got lucky.
When using AI, you have to make sure you are still outputting something that is up to the quality standards. We tend to expect computers to be smarter/more competent than we are- I think most of us have typed something in Word and gotten a spell check error, and even if you were pretty confident you spelled it correctly you probably got out your dictionary to check. It is easy to think what the computer puts out is always better than what you would create; at an extreme this can include even things like trusting information you get from generative AI more than an expert, but it can also include not being as thorough when you fact check things you get from AI because it feels more trustworthy. This is part of why you have to check any information with verified sources (also because the little robots don’t understand what a ‘fact’ is). If a model produces a bad result (e.g. bad image, inaccurate transcript, inaccurate information), you are responsible for anything that happens if you use it. AI is a tool and you are using it, it is not autonomous (yet)
While it’s less obvious when it’s on the cloud (on someone else’s computer), some AI models (generative AI in particular) use more resources than other options, and this sometimes translates to a higher cost to you. To be effective, you should balance your benefit with the increased resource use and try to find ways to reduce the increased resource use where possible.

title: AI Policy
text:
Practical Advice for Policies

Be prepared for technical issues
Adapt for your organization
Consider how this might fit with other policies
AI is not always traditional software
Try to provide alternatives


If you have an AI incident, there is a chance it will be because someone accidentally signed up for an AI tool, or tried to disconnect it from their calendar but had a technical issue. You might be thinking that the way ETDD does things sounds like the best method, but if it involves any paperwork it will absolutely not happen at your district. If so, figure out how to adapt for your organization. You probably already have a tech policy, but your tech policy almost certainly does not cover everything you need. AI is often not just on your computer, most of the user-facing options are also online, so a policy about downloading things will have low relevance If you have to say no to something, try to understand the need to find a suitable alternative. That’ll help address genuine needs and help improve buy-in to the policy.

title: AI 101
text:
Goals for the Training


Understand at a basic level what AI is
Understand that there are different kinds of AI models
Understand different AI models are appropriate for different tasks
Be able to apply this information to evaluate if an AI model is probably appropriate
Understand why the term AI has become seemingly ubiquitous
Have a basis for further learning (come back for AI 102! I’ll be a nerd and tell you why AI is cool!)

title: AI 101
text:
How would you define AI?




“AI is whatever hasn’t been done yet.” — Tesler’s Theorem1




Larry Tesler was a computer scientist who worked in the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (and later pioneered copy and paste as we know them today)

title: AI 101
text:
Defining AI

How do you define “Intelligence”?
Academic definitions differ and are not always helpful for practical use



AI Definition Overview2








Human-based
Ideal Rationality




Reasoning-based
Systems that think like humans
Systems that think rationally


Behavior-based
Systems that act like humans
Systems that act rationally





In general, it’s somewhat common to not have clear definitions in academic fields because it’s difficult to create a definition that covers everything it should while excluding everything else. As an exercise after this presentation, try to define a ‘chair’ in a way that would include e.g. beanbag chairs, but not horses AI does have a harder time with the definition of the field in part because as a field it interacts with computer science, statistics, philosophy, neurology, neurocomputation, and other fields. Depending on one’s focus, AI can be interpreted in wildly different ways, and that’s without accounting for academic vs common-use definitions.

title: AI 101
text:
Defining AI
AI is “an engineered or machine-based system that can, for a given set of objectives, generate outputs such as predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. AI systems are designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy”3

This is the definition we’re using at ETDD

title: AI 101
text:
Defining AI
Questions to help determine if it’s AI:

Does the technology use data to provide predictions, recommendations, insights, or decisions?
Does the technology augment or replace human decision-making?
Does the company use words such as “personalized”, “tailored”, and “adaptive” in its marketing?”
Would it be difficult or impossible to write out how a program does what it does, either because the process is opaque or because it is mathematically complex? 4


These are in our AI policy. If the answer is yes to any of them, you should treat it as AI until told otherwise.

Examples of AI



What are some examples of AI you interact with on a regular basis?




 Optical Character Recognition

How AI Works Conceptually


Understanding the different kinds can help you know when and how to use AI

When thinking of the different kinds of AI, it might help to think of them like Excel vs Word. You probably don’t know much about how either works at a low level (i.e. what language they’re coded in, how the data is represented), but you know you probably shouldn’t use Word to create tables with complex calculations or Excel to write an essay.

title: AI 101
section: Real Life Examples of Each Kind of AI
text:
Real Life Examples of Each Kind of AI




Logicist


Probabilistic


Neurocomputational






 Optical Character Recognition




 Video Games7



 Spam Filters8




Lots of video games use AI to control NPCs (non-playable characters). For example, in Skyrim NPCs have to know where to walk without someone controlling them, how do they do that? How do NPCs know how much they should charge for a book, or spend on a sword you want to sell them? Newer video games often use a combination of several kinds of AI, so perhaps in Skyrim there would be a logicist AI to determine where NPCs can walk, and then a neurocomputational AI to determine where they actually walk.
Unfortunately, spam emails don’t usually tell you they’re spam, so how can spam filters work? It would take hours and hours of manual work to create rules that would work for the spam I get on my work email and my personal email, much less that would be mostly accurate for most people. This is somewhere ML excels- select an appropriate model, give it labelled data, and it can create rules to catch most spam.
Optical Character Recognition is used to e.g. add words to scanned PDFs. It’s usually based on Neural nets, which are especially good at computer vision

title: AI 101
text:
AI Model Type Overview









Logicist
Probabilistic
Neurocomputational




good for:
clearly defined logic
clear factors
No clear process


bad at:
handling uncertainty
operating with limited /bad data or assumptions
Truth; Explainability


example:
email inbox rules
(some) population forecasting
chatGPT




To give another example of each: In my personal life, I’ve signed up for a few store rewards cards. I like looking at the flyers before I go, but I don’t want them cluttering up my inbox, so I set inbox rules to move those to a folder. Some of the companies use different emails, so I can’t create rules that always filter correctly because there is some uncertainty about what emails should / should not be moved.
At my last job, I had to predict how many cars we’d sell of certain types so we could buy appropriate stock. There aren’t clear, human definable rules for predicting that, but we had previous sales data. The data sucked, so the forecast had an upper limit on how good it could be. As a company we artificially inflated demand in December by running sales then, so we had to account for that outside of the Machine Learning model because the data was not accurate.
When I was a lab manager, one of the projects I worked on was creating an algorithm to sort hand-drawn paperwork. There were no clearly defined features I could use, and no clear rules about which was which I could communicate to a computer. I cross-trained a computer vision neural network to sort the documents into different folders but it couldn’t have told me e.g. why it sorted them a certain way.

title: AI 101
text:
Why AI Is Gaining Attention (again)

Why do you think there is a lot of hype around AI right now? Has this happened before?

title: AI 101
text:
Why not before now:

The idea of ‘artificial intelligence’ can be seen even in writings by Ancient Greek philosophers, so why haven’t robots taken over yet?
Turing’s Chess
early neural nets (1950’s)
Impracticality of probability
not user-friendly
AI Winters


AI as a field of study came from formal logic theory (which came from philosophy), with some important additions from probability theory and neuroscience.
Alan Turing created an AI which could play chess by designing an algorithm which would take the current state of a chess board, and then assign points to all possible next moves and choose the move with the highest points. Because of technical limitations, he never ran this on a computer.
Early neural nets had to be written by hand on punchcards- if you messed up somewhere in the middle you had to completely start over. There’s a hard upper limit on how much you can do in a system like that based on how many people you can have work on the project.
Until recently, probability-based AI was impractical for most cases. In a lot of situations, you’d need to run a few hundred calculations for a basic model, and gathering the data and running those calculations was difficult.
Typically, if you wanted cool AI tools you’d have to find/hire a nerd to make them for you. Most of the closer to off-the-shelf options required technical expertise to correctly use.
AI history is full of booms and busts- we figure out something cool and then over promise and under deliver. So people lose interest in AI because they think all of it is overhyped instead of just some, and then things that objectively are AI stop being called AI. This probably includes things you use every day, but don’t think of as AI.

title: AI 101
text:
Why now is different (technical):

Graphical Processing Units (GPUs)9
Incredible access to data

Raised some new needs

Bayesian probability is easy(ish) now
Attention Is All You Need10


GPUS are designed to run multiple processes at the same time, so models that would be too complex to run with CPUs because of the time became practical with GPUs
The increase in access to data is due to many factors, including the normalization of data collection e.g. by grocery stores and smart phones, but the easiest to point out is the rise of the internet. It’s a lot easier to scrape a few dozen websites and get usable data compared to trying to convert printed books into a format usable by ML. These changes have also created new (or amplified existing) use cases- like Netflix and Amazon wanting to show you things you might want to watch/buy.
Bayesian probability can handle uncertainty better, and is often more useful for ML than frequentist methods (come to AI 102 for a crash course), but requires a lot of computation which was impractical before modern computing.
A paper put out by computer scientists at Google in 2017 introduced a new way of adding context to things as they were processed by neural nets. Come to AI 102 if you want to learn more, it’s super cool, but the important part is that it created huge advances in the field

title: AI 101
text:
Why now is different (social):

chatbots (seem more human)11
Easy to use products
$$$
hype


Image generators and neural nets have existed for years now- back when I was in college there was a Tumblr blog that was a bot someone trained on their own blog (inspired by a webcomic) that ran from 2019-2024. The bot was fun, and was basically a research project by the person who developed it that took many hours to set up, and many more to keep working long term. Until chatGPT, advanced ML models were not available to casual users except in end products. Most casual users had access to things like google translate, but probably didn’t know it was AI much less anything about how it worked. Honestly, most people still don’t know anything about how the AI in their lives work, but it is less intimidating now that you can go to a website and “chat” with a bot instead of having to learn basic coding and already know that machine learning exists. We’re in the middle of a boom of AI- lots of novel research is being done and right now the public is more aware that AI can do cool things, so it’s cool to call things AI. Some of this is hype by people who are excited about AI, or are taking advantage of those who are excited, and this hype (and again, the genuinely good research) means there’s more money going to AI right now.

title: AI 101
text:
Why now is (not) different

upper limit on benefits from data
better compute still has a practical limit 12
putting all of the eggs in one or two baskets funding and hype-wise13


We’re already seeing decreasing returns on just adding more data, and now we’ve got issues where internet data is increasingly poisoned in a way that could lead to model collapse. Most of the hype around AI is around LLMs and image generators, which are cool and also are not magic. There’s a good chance as a society we will put all of our eggs into the ‘LLMs are going to save us’ basket, and in a few years when we still have jobs people will lose interest. Right now better computing has an upper limit in terms of resources available, and quantum computing is unlikely to save us soon (and poses its own issues)

title: AI 101
text:
Practical Concerns with AI

What do you think are some practical concerns when you are using AI? What do you try to keep in mind when you are using AI?

title: AI 101
text:
Genuine Dangers

Malware14
Legal Grey Areas

AI Notetakers15
State laws
Mostly applying existing laws to AI

Lost of trust with communities
Loss of quality


Generative AI is subject to unique ways of hacking that traditional software is not, and cybersecurity experts are just starting to get a sense of the scope of the problem. One area of particular interest is with coding- if you use generative AI to code there are several ways it can pose cybersecurity risks that are elevated compared to coding by hand. Even if you don’t open CoPilot and ask it to code for you, if you use low or no code tools, most of those use generative AI. The legal realm around AI is currently rapidly changing and uncertain. A lot of where we’re currently seeing these changes is with figuring out how current laws apply to new(er) AI tools. For example, multiple AI Notetakers are in court trying to determine things like if collecting biometric information without proper consent is breaking California privacy law, or if sending an AI notetaker to a meeting you don’t attend might be breaking federal wire tap law. You could potentially use AI in a way that leads to a loss of trust with our communities, for example if you use generative AI to create incorrect statistics and use those in a grant and don’t get the grant as a result, our communities might not want you to apply for grants any more. Due to automation bias, it’s really easy to produce worse-quality material (e.g. a grant application) that you feel is better because it came from a more automated source.

title: AI 101
text:
Ethics

Data Collection

Privacy Rights16

Copyright

Potential loss of models17
Currently not always able to copyright AI generated content in the US18

Public Opinion

Plagiarism19



In Data Science, a common refrain is garbage in garbage out. At an extreme, if a model was trained on 4chan, you’re not going to get professional emails out of it. For a more realistic example, if a transcript generating model was only trained on men with british accents, it won’t do a good job transcribing meetings we’re in. Data collection can also affect how the company uses your data- e.g. most AI note takers use your data to train their models, which makes them not appropriate for meetings where someone might say something you don’t want in a model you can’t control.
There are currently several court cases working to determine if training AI on copyrighted works without permission is considered fair use, and if there are instances where it might not be. Those cases could significantly impact many of the popular models today.
Right now, public opinion is still highly polarized on AI. Some people have serious ethical concerns with how a lot of the larger LLM models have been trained or are being used (e.g. to replace workers), while others consider any use of AI to be inherently unethical. Navigating this landscape will require us to be aware of opinions and considerate of them even if we disagree with them (both pro- and anti- AI).

title: AI 101
text:
Right model for the job

Probabilistic vs deterministic
Is AI the best solution?
Resource use

Less obvious when it’s “on the cloud”, but still worth considering



If I create a website for online shopping, and you judge it based on whether or not it lists states with the letter “R” in their name, that’s not really fair unless my website is the “states with the letter”R” in their name shop”
If you need specific wording or existing data, probabilistic AI is not going to be appropriate. If you want to organize pdfs, probabilistic AI is more likely to work (unless you have a good file naming scheme) because it can create rules to determine which files are similar to one another.
There are times where AI is not the best solution. If you want to check the accessibility of a website, there is a lot you can do with automation (e.g. color contrast), but some of it is easier to just check than try to create AI for (full screen-reader compatibility).
Certain AI models do require more resources to use, you should balance how much you benefit from those models with the resources required to use them.

title: AI 101
section: You are the one using the tool
text:
You are the one using the tool

Automation Bias20
Responsibility


As a general rule, people tend to trust or rely on things that are ‘automated’ more than they should. This can include even things like trusting information you get from AI more than an expert because most LLMs are really good at sounding confident even when they’re wrong (like a lot of people on the internet), but it can also include not being as thorough when you fact check things you get from AI because it feels more trustworthy. This is part of why you have to check any information with verified sources (also because the little robots don’t understand what a ‘fact’ is). If a model produces a bad result (e.g. bad image, inaccurate transcript, inaccurate information), you are responsible for anything that happens if you use it. AI is a tool and you are using it, it is not autonomous (yet)

title: AI 101
text:
Quick Practical Advice

Don’t use AI search results without verifying them
If you use chat bots:

Give them context in your prompt
Try different prompting strategies (e.g. say “you are an X”, be nice)

Don’t forget the sunk cost fallacy
Sometimes older methods are better


The AI search is unreliable, you need to verify any information with reliable sources (like in our policy)
Currently, there is not clear evidence that certain strategies are universally helpful with all tasks for any given LLM model, and the models are changing at a fast enough pace that a strategy that works now might not a month from now. Instead, try different strategies to see which seems to get you what you want. Some strategies include being polite in how you word the prompt, including in your prompt something like “You are a planner,”, and prompting it to “think step by step”. This last one will use more resources, and is not always effective, so use it sparingly.
It can be easy to spend more time trying to do something an ‘easy’ way than you would doing it the ‘hard’ way. I have a tendency to do this, and what I’ve found helpful is setting a timer for a quarter or half as long as I think it’d take to do the hard way and switching to the hard way if I’m not done in that time.
AI is the new cool shiny toy that’s suddenly everywhere, and it can be exciting to use new tech (or old tech with a new coat of paint). It’s not always the most effective or easiest way to do things even if it feels like it is.

title: AI 101
text: Putting It In Action / Hypotheticals

title: AI 101
text:
Let’s say you want to predict the future population of Knoxville. A company is offering to use AI to forecast the population for the next ten years. What could you ask them to evaluate if their methods work?
Would you feel confident in your answer, or would you want to ask someone who is more familiar with forecasting?

Let’s say you want to write Planning 101 guide for ETDD to give to local planners to help them make better choices (and maybe sell later), but you’re on a time crunch so you want to use a generative AI to help you.
How could it help you?
What concerns would you keep in mind using it?

title: AI 101
text:
What we’ll cover:

What AI is
Examples of AI in your life
How AI works (conceptually)
Why AI is gaining attention (again)
Practical concerns when using AI
Examples of how to put this information into action

title: AI 101
text:
Goals for the Training


Understand at a basic level what AI is
Understand that there are different kinds of AI models
Understand different AI models are appropriate for different tasks
Be able to apply this information to evaluate if an AI model is probably appropriate
Understand why the term AI has become seemingly ubiquitous
Have a basis for further learning (come back for AI 102! I’ll be a nerd and tell you why AI is cool!)

title: AI 101
text:
How would you define AI?




“AI is whatever hasn’t been done yet.” — Tesler’s Theorem1




Larry Tesler was a computer scientist who worked in the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (and later pioneered copy and paste as we know them today)

title: AI 101
text:
Defining AI

How do you define “Intelligence”?
Academic definitions differ and are not always helpful for practical use



AI Definition Overview2








Human-based
Ideal Rationality




Reasoning-based
Systems that think like humans
Systems that think rationally


Behavior-based
Systems that act like humans
Systems that act rationally





In general, it’s somewhat common to not have clear definitions in academic fields because it’s difficult to create a definition that covers everything it should while excluding everything else. As an exercise after this presentation, try to define a ‘chair’ in a way that would include e.g. beanbag chairs, but not horses AI does have a harder time with the definition of the field in part because as a field it interacts with computer science, statistics, philosophy, neurology, neurocomputation, and other fields. Depending on one’s focus, AI can be interpreted in wildly different ways, and that’s without accounting for academic vs common-use definitions.

title: AI 101
text:
Defining AI
AI is “an engineered or machine-based system that can, for a given set of objectives, generate outputs such as predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. AI systems are designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy”3

This is the definition we’re using at ETDD

title: AI 101
text:
Defining AI
Questions to help determine if it’s AI:

Does the technology use data to provide predictions, recommendations, insights, or decisions?
Does the technology augment or replace human decision-making?
Does the company use words such as “personalized”, “tailored”, and “adaptive” in its marketing?”
Would it be difficult or impossible to write out how a program does what it does, either because the process is opaque or because it is mathematically complex? 4


These are in our AI policy. If the answer is yes to any of them, you should treat it as AI until told otherwise.

title: AI 101
text:
Examples of AI



What are some examples of AI you interact with on a regular basis?




 Optical Character Recognition


 Video Games5


 Spam Filters6

title: AI 101
text:
How AI Works Conceptually


Understanding the different kinds can help you know when and how to use AI










G
